title: Systematic construction of quantum many-body scars in frustrated Rydberg arrays
authors: Jean-Yves Desaules, Aron Kerschbaumer, Marko Ljubotina, Maksym Serbyn
abstract: Quantum many-body scars in Rydberg atom arrays have thus far only been observed on bipartite lattices, leaving open the question of whether and how they survive frustration, and what the appropriate initial states are that lead to nonthermal dynamics. We introduce a graph-theoretic framework to find suitable candidates for scarring on arbitrary lattices. Our framework predicts two distinct mechanisms: type-I scars generalize the bipartite case by using locally entangled states to overcome mild frustration, while type-II scars exploit strong frustration to pin part of the lattice, leaving the remainder to oscillate freely. We numerically demonstrate both mechanisms and uncover an exponential family of scarred trajectories on the hexagonal lattice that can encode information protected from thermalization. Our results establish scarring as a generic feature of Rydberg systems beyond one dimension and provide an experimentally accessible route to systematically probing non-thermal dynamics in quantum simulators.
summary: This paper presents a new graph-theoretic method to find and classify quantum many-body scars in frustrated Rydberg atom arrays. It identifies two distinct mechanisms—Type-I and Type-II—that allow non-thermal dynamics to persist even in non-bipartite lattices. The results offer a roadmap for using complex lattice geometries to control and stabilize many-body quantum dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating whether and how quantum many-body scars (QMBS) survive in frustrated, non-bipartite Rydberg atom arrays and identifying the initial states that lead to non-thermal dynamics.
main_result: The authors introduce a graph-theoretic framework that identifies two distinct scarring mechanisms: Type-I (using local entanglement to overcome mild frustration) and Type-II (using strong frustration to pin part of the lattice).
method: A graph-theoretic approach using vertex partitioning and clique covers, combined with algebraic construction of an approximate su(2) algebra and numerical simulations of return fidelity.
model_or_system: Rydberg atom arrays in various 2D and 3D geometries (e.g., hexagonal, Shastry-Sutherland, asanoha, kagome, and triangular lattices) described by the PXP model in the blockade regime.
key_observables: Return fidelity, entanglement entropy, sublattice occupation, and overlap with dimer Neel states.
important_parameters: Levels of lattice frustration, connectivity/coordination numbers of sublattices, and driving frequency adjustments.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that the dynamics can be captured by an approximate su(2) algebra and that the lattice can be partitioned into specific sublattices (A, B, and C).
figures_summary: Fig 1: Shastry-Sutherland lattice partitioning and return fidelity. Fig 2: Type-I scarring in hexagonal lattices with different dimer covers. Fig 3: Type-II scarring in the asanoha lattice. Fig 4: Ruby lattice dynamics and frequency-stabilization strategy. Fig 5: Type-II scarring in pyrochlore lattices. Fig 6: Comparison of scarring robustness in 1D chains, PXP ladders, and modified lattices. Fig 7: Comparison of scarring in Kagome, FCS, and triangular lattices.
paper_structure: Introduction of the problem, presentation of the graph-theoretic framework, detailed analysis of Type-I scarring mechanisms, analysis of Type-II scarring mechanisms, investigation of stability and robustness in various geometries, and discussion of experimental implications.
why_interesting: It provides a systematic, dimension-insensitive way to engineer non-thermal dynamics in complex, frustrated geometries, which is highly relevant for designing quantum simulators and protecting information from thermalization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Type-I scarring in the Shastry-Sutherland lattice on a torus with 36 spins. (a) Dimer cover and (b) effective square lattice of these dimers. Atoms in A (B) are in blue (red). (c) Entanglement entropy of eigenstates in the fully symmetric sector along the cut shown in (a). (d) Overlap of the dimer N´eel state shown in (a) with the eigenstates in the fully sym- metric sector. States highlighted in red in (c) and (d) are the same and are those with maximal overlap in their en- ergy range. While the scarred structure is clearly visible in panel (d), there is no band of well-separated eigenstates. As a consequence, no clear outliers are visible in the entangle- ment entropy, making it...

FIG. 2. Type-I scarring in the hexagonal lattice on a torus with Nx=4, Ny=4 and 32 spins. (a)-(i) The 9 bipartite (out of 449) dimer covers. (a) Vertical stripe cover. (b)-(i) The 2Ny−1=8 different zigzag dimer covers. Black arrows indicate which rows are shifted. (j) Fidelity after quenches from the initial states shown in (a)-(i), the N´eel and all down states.

FIG. 3. Type-II scarring in the quasi-2D asanoha lattice. (a) The lattice is a triangular lattice made of tetrahedra that point up and down alternatively. (b) Lattice with 36 spins used for the numerical simulations in (c) and (d). (c) Fidelity after a quench from two different initial product states. (d) Occupation nj = (1 + σz j )/2 after a quench from the ground state of Jy. The occupation of sites in C is highly suppressed, while A and B oscillate almost freely.

FIG. 5. Type-II scarring in the quasi-2D pyrochlore lattice. (a) The lattice is a kagome lattice of tetrahedra, with tetra- hedra pointing up and down alternately. (b) Lattice with 40 spins used for the numerical simulations in (c) and (d). (c) Return fidelity after quenches from |GSZ⟩and |GSY ⟩and from the deformed states. (d)-(e) Sublattice occupation after a quench from (d) |GSY ⟩and (e) |Yγ=0.7⟩. The occupation of C is suppressed, but still shows some oscillations.

FIG. 4. (a) Triangle cover in the ruby lattice and (b) corre- sponding effective hexagonal lattice. (c) Vertex-dimer cover and (d) corresponding effective lattice. The squares in (d) correspond to dimers, while crosses correspond to single ver- tices in the original lattice. (e)-(f) Fidelity after quenches from various initial states in a system with 24 sites, see inset of (f). The maximally occupied (Max. occ.) state is one of the basis states with the most up-spins. With a homogeneous Hamiltonian, the best revivals are seen from the N´eel state of the triangle cover, while in the frequency-adjusted Hamil- tonian, the vertex-dimer N´eel state displays the best fidelity instead.
